q: The environment is the surroundings comprising of hydrosphere, lithosphere, atmosphere and biosphere.
The pathways that allow different chemical substances to move through living and non-living forms and their locations in the biosphere are called a biogeochemical cycle.
The cycling of elements is interconnected with the water cycle.
Mineral nutrients are cycled, either rapidly or slowly, through the entire biosphere and from one living organism to another.
Most of the Earth’s carbon is stored in the atmosphere.
The rest of the Earth's carbon is located in the ocean, atmosphere, and in living organisms.
Plants absorb carbon dioxide during photosynthesis.
Plants release carbon dioxide when they decay.
Respiration is the process of gas exchange between the atmosphere and living organisms.
In the fossilisation process, the animal and plant residues become sediment and eventually turn into rock and minerals.
The carbon is stored as coal and other fossil fuels in rocks and other geological deposits.
The combustion of fossil fuels and organic materials gives off carbon dioxide, water, and energy.

The nitrogen cycle is a biogeochemical process through which nitrogen is converted into many forms, consecutively passing from the atmosphere to the soil and then to organisms, and back into the atmosphere.
The nitrogen cycle involves the following steps: nitrogen fixation, nitrification, nitrogen assimilation, ammonification, and denitrification.
Unlike many other cycling elements, phosphorus can be found in the air.
Phosphorus is mainly cycling through water, soil and sediments.
Phosphorus is an essential nutrient for plants and animals in the form of ions $PO_{4}^{3-}$ and $HPO_{4}^{2-}$.
Phosphorus is also a building block of certain parts of the human including DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) and animal bodies, such as bones and teeth.
Phosphate salts that are released from rocks through weathering usually dissolve in water present in soil and will be absorbed by plants.
The phosphorus cycle moves through plants and animals much faster than it does through rocks and sediments.
Marine birds play a unique role in the phosphorus cycle, as they take phosphorus-containing fish out of the ocean and return to land, where they defecate.
Guano contains a high level of phosphorus and in this way, marine birds return phosphorus from the ocean to the land.
Sulphur is one of the components that make up proteins and vitamins.
Animals absorb sulphur when sulphur-containing compounds are dissolved in water.
Most of the Earth's sulphur is found in rocks and salts or buried deep in the ocean in oceanic sediments.
Natural sources of sulphur in the atmosphere include volcanic eruptions, bacterial processes, or decaying organisms.
When sulphur dioxide enters the atmosphere, it will react with oxygen to produce sulphur trioxide gas ($SO_{3}$), or with other chemicals in the atmosphere, to produce sulphur salts.
Sulphuric acid may also be produced from dimethyl sulphide emitted to the atmosphere by plankton species.
Ocean acidification refers to a reduction in the pH of the ocean over an extended period of time, caused primarily by the uptake of carbon dioxide from the atmosphere.
Ocean acidification interferes with the ability of marine organisms (including corals, Dungeness crabs, and snails) to build their shells and skeletons.
Excess nitrogen and phosphorus that enter the ecosystem from fertiliser runoff and from sewage cause excessive growth of algae.
Algal blooms block light and therefore kill aquatic plants in rivers, lakes, and seas.
The subsequent death and decay of aquatic plants deplete dissolved oxygen, which leads to the death of aquatic organisms.
The extraction of metals such as copper, lead, and zinc from sulphur-containing ores releases sulphur dioxide.
Acid rain damages the natural environment by lowering the pH of lakes, thus killing many of the resident plants and aquatic animals.
